metadata.summary: Framework for centralizing business metrics into a single, actionable view. Dashboard design principles, metric selection, data connections, and reporting cadences.

content.title: Dashboard Setup
content.summary: A framework for centralizing business metrics into a single, actionable view. Covers dashboard design principles, metric selection (5-10 max), data source connections, and reporting cadences (daily pulse, weekly review, monthly report). The core rule: every metric on a dashboard must be one you can act on — lagging vanity metrics belong in archives, not on the dashboard.

why.p: Metrics scattered across tools (Stripe, Google Analytics, CRM, product analytics) create decision lag. A single dashboard surface reduces time-to-insight from hours to seconds, enables pattern detection across correlated metrics (e.g., CAC vs LTV), and makes weekly reviews actionable rather than archaeological.

list.item: No single view of business health exists across revenue, customers, and marketing
Weekly review meetings waste time hunting for numbers across tools
Setting up a new SaaS or product business that needs an initial metrics system
Quarterly planning requires trend visibility across multiple metric categories
Switching analytics tools and need to rebuild reporting infrastructure
Business has fewer than 10 customers — a simple spreadsheet outperforms a dashboard
Metrics are undefined or inconsistently tracked — fix data collection first
No one will review the dashboard regularly — a stale dashboard is worse than none
Dashboard tool requires data warehouse setup that hasn't been built yet

rule.statement: Include 5-10 key metrics maximum on any single dashboard. A dashboard with 30 metrics is a report, not a decision tool.
rule.rationale: Too many metrics dilute attention. A dashboard should answer the question "is my business healthy today?" in under 5 seconds. More than 10 metrics prevents this glance-level assessment.

list.item: Glanceable: status visible in 5 seconds without analysis
Focused: 5-10 key metrics maximum
Current: real-time or daily updates (not monthly)
Actionable: only metrics you can directly influence
Comparative: show trend vs prior period, not just point-in-time value

rule.statement: Build separate dashboards for daily pulse, weekly review, and monthly report — each has a different audience, update frequency, and decision type.
rule.rationale: A daily dashboard optimized for speed cannot serve monthly analysis. Combining them produces a hybrid that does neither well.

list.item: Daily pulse: quick health check, real-time or daily updates, 5-7 metrics
Weekly review: trend analysis, weekly update, 10-12 metrics
Monthly report: full analysis, monthly update, full metric set
Real-time: live monitoring for time-sensitive operations

rule.statement: Include only metrics that pass the actionability test: if this metric changes 20% in the wrong direction, what specifically would you do differently?
rule.rationale: Lagging vanity metrics (total pageviews, total followers) feel important but produce no decision. Actionable metrics (conversion rate, CAC, churn rate) map to specific interventions.

list.item: MRR (Monthly Recurring Revenue): total recurring revenue normalized to a month
MRR Growth: (current MRR - prior MRR) / prior MRR × 100%
New MRR: revenue from new customers this month
Churned MRR: revenue lost from canceled customers
Expansion MRR: additional revenue from upgrades and add-ons
Net New MRR: New MRR + Expansion MRR - Churned MRR
Total customers: count of active paying customers
New customers: signups that converted to paid this month
Churned customers: customers who canceled this month
Churn rate: churned / total at start of period × 100%
LTV (Lifetime Value): ARPU / monthly churn rate
CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost): total sales + marketing spend / new customers
LTV:CAC ratio: target 3:1 minimum, 8:1 healthy for SaaS
CAC payback period: CAC / (ARPU × gross margin) in months
Organic visitors: monthly visits from search and direct
Signup rate: signups / visitors × 100% (target: 2-5%)
Activation rate: activated users / signups × 100% (target: 40%+)
Trial-to-paid conversion: paid conversions / trial starts × 100% (target: 20-40%)
Cost per acquisition (CPA): spend / conversions per channel

rule.statement: Track each funnel metric per acquisition channel (organic, paid, referral, direct). Channel-level metrics reveal where to invest; blended metrics hide the signal.
rule.rationale: A 3% overall conversion rate may be 8% for organic and 0.5% for paid. Blended metrics would suggest the paid channel is contributing; channel breakdown reveals it is destroying ROI.

rule.statement: Review dashboards on a fixed cadence: daily pulse check (2 min), weekly review (30 min), monthly report (2 hours). Skip reviews create metric blindspots.
rule.rationale: The purpose of a dashboard is to catch problems early. A dashboard reviewed monthly catches problems a month after they start. A dashboard reviewed daily catches them on day 2.

list.item: Daily: pulse check — is anything broken? (revenue, signups, errors)
Weekly: trend review — are key metrics moving in the right direction?
Monthly: full analysis — channel performance, cohort comparison, targets vs actuals
Quarterly: target reset — update benchmarks based on current trajectory

rule.statement: Set automated alerts for metric deviations greater than 20% from the 7-day rolling average. Alert on drops, not just on targets.
rule.rationale: Target-based alerts ("below $X MRR") miss sudden drops that remain above target. Deviation-based alerts catch anomalies regardless of absolute level.

template.purpose: Spreadsheet structure: revenue, customers, unit economics, marketing
Dashboard specification: purpose, audience, metrics, visualizations
Monthly report template: executive summary, KPIs, channel performance
